For example, Polysights provides a service similar to a "prediction market Bloomberg terminal," aggregating decentralized data from platforms such as Polymarket, Kalshi, and Limitless to track smart money movements and generate advanced arbitrage signals. In the betting and sports sectors, Azuro has demonstrated the opportunity of "backend as a service," with its protocol packaging liquidity pools, odds engines, and sports data oracles (Chainlink), allowing new projects to focus on user experience and GTM promotion. Meanwhile, cross-chain liquidity and order aggregation become key, with markets distributed across Base (Limitless), Polygon (Polymarket), and Solana (Melee). Flipr aggregates order flows, routes odds, and resolves cross-chain settlements, playing the role of "1 inch in the prediction market." In addition, the opportunity for GTM and interaction layer middleware lies in embedded tools, such as Flipr and Noise, which inject trading functions (such as one-click ordering and leverage) into Telegram Bots, X platform tweets, or content wallets, directly capturing traffic and trading behavior the moment a user generates trading intent. (II) Comparison of Leading Projects (Image 1) North American Market: Compliance and Data-Driven (Image 2) Compliance-Driven Financial Dominance (Kalshi): Kalshi, relying on CFTC... Licenses dominate the US compliance market, monopolizing the trading of regulated macroeconomic data such as economic indicators and Federal Reserve interest rate decisions, with its market share once surpassing Polymarket by 60%. Political and Event-Driven Divergence (Dual Monopoly): High-profile political events are dominated by Polymarket (global funds) and Kalshi (US compliant funds), forming a clear regional and financial segmentation. Avoiding Platform Competition (Extremely High GTM): The barriers to entry for leading platforms are already established; blindly establishing new compliant exchanges is too costly and risky. The idea of ​​directly challenging Kalshi/Polymarket should be abandoned. Capitalizing on the "Supporting Layer" (Tool Opportunities): The real opportunity lies in serving giants, such as developing front-end analytics tools for Robinhood/Kalshi channels (Polysights model), or becoming a distributor of institutional compliant data.

2Global/Offshore Market: Sports as a Basic Need and Product Shortage

  • Sports as a Basic Need (Largest Vertical): Sports is the most certain growth track globally. 92% of Kalshi's recent incremental growth comes from sports. The high TVL of on-chain protocols such as Azuro/Overtime verifies its offshore basic need attribute.

  • Middleware Strategy (Shovel Business): Investing in liquidity and odds middleware like Azuro is the best strategy. They provide the underlying infrastructure for all front-end sports betting apps, enabling "plug and play".

  • Middleware Strategy (Shovel Business): Investing in liquidity and odds middleware like Azuro is the best strategy. They provide the underlying infrastructure for all front-end sports betting apps, enabling "plug and play".

  • Solving the "Missing Piece" Pain Point: Leading platforms lack the "Parlay" function due to compliance and risk aversion. Platforms that can implement this function in a decentralized manner will have a huge siphon effect on highly engaged gambling users. Crypto-Native Short-Term Gambling (Limitless): High-frequency crypto price fluctuation prediction for DeFi traders (such as the Limitless model) is another essential niche market, satisfying short-term speculative needs. 3. Asian Market: Social Spread and Long-Tail Micro-Markets Mobile-First and Social-Driven (Market Characteristics): The Asian market is an underdeveloped blind spot. Users prefer mobile operation, social sharing, and high-frequency interaction, which is very different from the institutionalized style in Europe and the United States. Abandon grand political narratives (localization): Asian users lack interest in and are sensitive to macro-political issues in Europe and America, so the strategy of replicating Polymarket's political betting should be completely abandoned. Telegram bots are king (best entry point): Build a lightweight entry point based on Telegram bots (such as Flipr, okbet), greatly reducing the entry barrier and perfectly matching the social and payment habits of Asian users. Micro-level entertainment topics (long-tail strategy): Focusing on long-tail micro-events such as e-sports, KOL gossip, and meme pricing, utilizing Melee-style "creator revenue sharing" mechanisms to stimulate KOLs' self-promotion and viral growth. (See the image below for the federal regulatory framework.) alt="4GVdzHLglX8NeBfbjzu8A6I66Fw1CvYrfPKbEfLo.png">

    State Level Regulatory Status

    1The most intense battleground in the US's "federal" vs. "state" jurisdictional struggle, the outcome of which will have a decisive impact on the industry. ... Kalshi and the CFTC argue that the Commodity Exchange Act has exclusive federal jurisdiction, claiming that their "event contracts" are financial derivatives and should take precedence over state law. However, gaming commissions in at least eight states, including New York, argue that this is merely regulatory arbitrage, a "backdoor" to circumvent state-level gaming licenses and taxes. This controversy is not only a debate over the interpretation of legal provisions but also a struggle between regulatory power and fiscal interests. The verdict in the Kalshi case will be a watershed moment: a victory for Kalshi would clear the biggest obstacle to federal financial regulation; a defeat could force all prediction markets to accept fragmented gaming regulation dominated by 51 states, with unimaginably high compliance costs.

    2Case Study:Kalshi vs. NYGCLitigation

    The conflict between Kalshi and the New York State Gaming Commission stems from a core legal contradiction between the Commodity Exchange Act (federal law) and state gaming laws. The incident began when the New York State Gaming Commission issued a cease and desist letter to Kalshi, accusing it of offering unlicensed sports betting. Kalshi subsequently filed a lawsuit in federal court, arguing that federal law had exclusive jurisdiction over its event contracts and emphasizing that federal jurisdiction should take precedence over state gaming laws to avoid regulatory confusion caused by "51 different and conflicting state laws." The case became further complicated by tribal litigation, involving Kalshi's partner Robinhood, and California tribes also filed a lawsuit invoking the Indian Gaming Regulation Act (IGRA), expanding the jurisdictional issue to a triple dimension of federal, state, and tribal law, significantly increasing the legal and regulatory uncertainty of the case.

    ... The current state of regulation in various countries is seen as ineffective by regulators due to the "borderless" or "decentralized" nature upon which prediction markets (especially DeFi platforms) rely. Regulators in various countries are actively using geo-blocking, payment channel blocking, and ISP bans to enforce their domestic jurisdiction. The global (non-US) regulatory trend towards prediction markets is **clearly tightening**. Regulators are not turning a blind eye to its "innovation"; instead, they are rapidly incorporating it into the two existing regulatory frameworks (finance or gaming) and are firmly rejecting overseas operators attempting to circumvent domestic licenses. Frankly, the current regulatory landscape is extremely chaotic and unification is virtually impossible in the short term. Different classifications will lead to drastically different compliance requirements and legal consequences. First, if classified as a commodity or derivative, the regulatory body is the federal-level CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission). The logic here is that the market is used to hedge against the risks of future events (such as interest rates and political events), functioning similarly to futures, serving price discovery and risk management. The Kalshi platform is striving to develop along this path and has already obtained CFTC authorization. Secondly, if classified as gambling or betting, the regulatory bodies include state betting commissions (such as the New York State Betting Commission) and international betting organizations (such as ANJ in France and UKGC in the UK). The logic is that the market is used to "bet" on the outcome of future events, primarily for entertainment rather than economic risk management. Real-world examples include New York State's accusation against Kalshi for offering unlicensed sports betting, and Belgium and other countries directly banning Polymarket. Finally, if classified as securities, the regulatory body is the federal-level SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission), primarily targeting DeFi platforms. When a platform's profit expectations depend on the continuous efforts of its core team or the underlying asset is linked to a stock price, it may trigger a Howey Test and be considered an investment contract. Real-world evidence is the SEC's continued focus on scrutinizing token issuance in DeFi prediction markets. (II) Mechanism Analysis: Business Model, Transaction Mechanism, and Oracles 1. Business Model Analysis Kalshi's Compliant Revenue Strategy: This platform leverages its regulatory license as a moat, employing a high-fee centralized model to directly capture B2C transaction revenue, aiming to dominate a compliant financial market trusted by institutions and conservative traders. Polymarket's Market Share Priority Strategy: The project utilizes capital support to maintain extremely low fees, prioritizing market share and data assets, building barriers through network effects, and focusing on unlocking the long-term data value of B2B DaaS. Limitless's Protocol Value Strategy: This platform adopts a DeFi-native model, using a fee-based token buyback economic flywheel to directly empower the protocol token with platform value, relying on token incentive mechanisms to compete for high-frequency liquidity in the crypto space. Cultivate Labs' Robust B2B Strategy: This model avoids speculative markets, focusing on providing SaaS solutions to support decision-making for large enterprises and governments, locking in predictable recurring revenue through strong customer relationships. Potential and Challenges of Hybrid Models: Embedded models, represented by Melee and Myriad, possess the largest potential market size by reaching a broad range of internet users, but still face the challenge of translating entertainment-driven forecasts into sustainable economic models.

2、Analysis of Trading Mechanisms: Liquidity, Matching, and Capital Efficiency

From theoretical models (CLOB, LMSR) to specific platform implementations (Polymarket, Kalshi, etc.), this paper systematically compares their differences in matching mechanisms, architecture, position sizing mechanisms, capital efficiency, and risk characteristics.

  • How to tradeoff between "efficiency" and "decentralization">

  • Capital efficiency (advantages of CEXs): Models like CLOB (order book) are extremely capital efficient, offering low slippage and deep liquidity.

  • Capital efficiency (advantages of CEXs): Models like CLOB (order book) are extremely capital efficient, offering low slippage and deep liquidity.

This is essential for attracting professional traders and high trading volumes. Decentralization (the soul of DeFi): Models like AMMs are permissionless and censorship-resistant. This is the core value of prediction markets as a "global information market." None of the current market leaders can perfectly achieve both simultaneously. They have all made compromises: Kalshi: 100% efficiency, 0% decentralization. (Completely embracing regulation and centralization) Gnosis/Omen: 100% decentralization, 0% efficiency. (LMSR is too inefficient, nobody uses it)

  • Polymarket: Hybrid compromise. (Trading efficiency for off-chain matching, but sacrificing some decentralization and introducing regulatory risks)

  • Limitless: Technical compromise. (Attempting to force an on-chain CLOB using L2 speed, but still relying on professional market makers)

    • The holy grail of the industry is finding a model that has both the efficiency of CLOB and the passive and decentralized characteristics of AMM. The "clumsiness" of the old AMM lies in its even distribution of liquidity across the entire range of 0% to 100%, resulting in 90% of funds being "dormant" in a 50/50 market, leading to extremely low efficiency. "Centralized liquidity" (the core concept of Uni v3) is the holy grail for resolving this contradiction, allowing liquidity providers (LPs) to "smartly" concentrate 100% of their funds in a high-probability trading range of 40%-60%. This new model perfectly combines two major advantages: it achieves CLOB-like capital efficiency and low slippage while retaining the decentralized essence of AMMs—permissionless and passively "set up and forget." Therefore, the future of the market is a "smart AMM" specifically tailored for prediction markets, with "centralized liquidity" at its core, and may use Uni v4's "Hooks" to add advanced features such as dynamic fees. 3. Oracle Mechanism: Trust, Security, and Settlement Efficiency of Outcome Decisions Authoritarian Epistemology: Centralized Regulatory Model (Kalshi) Mechanism: Based on legal rulebooks, the platform designates a single authoritative data source (such as a Department of Labor report) for decision-making. Source of Trust: Legal contracts and CFTC regulation. Advantages: High legal certainty, suitable for institutions. Limitations: The scope of adjudication is limited by regulatory permission (e.g., previously prohibited political predictions). Consensus Epistemology: Decentralized Schelling Point Model (Augur, UMA) Mechanism: Utilizing the Schelling point principle to incentivize token holders to vote on the outcome. **Augur:** REP token holders vote, triggering a fork in case of controversy (extremely slow and expensive). **UMA (Polymarket):** Optimistic oracle mode. Passes by default without controversy; disputes escalate to DVM voting. **Security Assumptions:** Corruption Cost (CoC) > Corruption Benefit (PfC). **Limitations:** Governance attack risk. The 2025 UMA incident in the Ukrainian market, where it was manipulated by whales, demonstrates the vulnerability of token voting to attacks with low participation.

    • Judicial Epistemology: Decentralized Arbitration Tribunal (Kleros)

    • Mechanism: Randomly select jurors to adjudicate complex/subjective disputes, similar to an on-chain court.

    • Advantages: Highly expressive, capable of handling ambiguous/unstructured disputes.

    • Limitations: High cost, slow speed.

    • Evolution of Emerging Mechanisms

    • Pyth Network (Pull Model)： Top-tier institutions directly sign on the blockchain, high frequency, low cost, suitable for objective price data (such as Limitless).

    • AI + TEE (Opinion/Buzzing)： AI Agent automatically captures/adjudicates, TEE hardware ensures the process is trustworthy, and the community provides a safety net. Solves the adjudication cost in long-tail markets.

    • AVSShared Security(EigenLayer)： Utilizes heavily staked ETH to provide economic security, mitigating the attack risk caused by insufficient market capitalization of a single oracle token.

    • Creator as Oracle(Melee)： Allows creators to customize rules and make decisions, adapting to the long-tail market of social/entertainment.


    • For protocol developers: Reject the "universal oracle," embrace specialization

    • Financial/price markets: Choose first-party data source models such as Pyth or Chainlink, pursuing ultimate performance and fidelity. Avoid using voting mechanisms to decide high-frequency prices.

    Objective/Binary Events: Employs an optimistic model based on EigenLayer AVS to ensure that the "cost of corruption" always outweighs the "gains of corruption." Subjective/Fuzzy Events: Integrates decentralized arbitration systems such as Kleros, or adopts Kalshi's centralized "rulebook" model. Long-Tail/Social Markets: Adopts Melee's "creator-oracle" model and clearly positions it as a social entertainment product. For bettors and investors, oracle risk is a core consideration. Primary Risk Control: Before trading, the market's adjudication mechanism must be read and fully understood. Define your bets: Ask yourself, "Am I betting on the outcome of the event, or on the voting behavior of a group of anonymous token holders?" The 2025 Polymarket attack proved that the two can be completely opposite. Risk pricing: For markets that rely on cryptoeconomic consensus, the risk of oracle manipulation must be factored into your odds and position models. The development of market oracles is a history of moving from idealism to pragmatism, and then towards specialization and modularity. The initial "truth machines" (such as Augur) failed due to their complexity and inefficiency. More pragmatic "optimistic models" (such as UMA) were dismantled due to their fatal flaw of isolated economic security. Regulated "authoritative models" (such as Kalshi) are technically robust, but their application is strictly limited. The future is neither entirely centralized nor purely decentralized, but a combination of hybrid and specialized approaches. Hybrid: Integrating AI automation (improving efficiency), human oversight (ensuring security), and the security of the sharing economy (addressing economic loopholes) to form a composite system of "human-machine loop." Specialized: Embracing specialized models like Pyth (specializing in price) and Melee (specializing in social interaction), which achieve their core value by sacrificing generality. Ultimately, the "oracle problem" will not be "solved" by a single mechanism, but rather "managed" by an evolving ecosystem comprised of multiple mechanisms. This ecosystem will provide different trade-offs for different types of "truth" based on four dimensions: security, speed, cost, and expressibility. V. Compliance as a Growth Anchor 2025 marks a paradigm shift in prediction markets from fringe experiments to financial infrastructure. Catalyzed by both capital and regulation, the sector has formed a duopoly of Kalshi (compliant finance) and Polymarket (offshore DeFi), with the business logic shifting from single transaction fees to B2B data distribution and embedded traffic monetization. Compliance has become the anchor of growth, and regulatory characterization will determine the market ceiling. The industry is gradually differentiating into institutionalized paths relying on licenses and censorship-resistant paths relying on code. Infrastructure is also developing professionally, with matching mechanisms evolving from AMM to CLOB to improve capital efficiency, and oracles upgrading from simple token voting to a hybrid defense system of "AI automation + AVS shared security" to cope with governance attack risks. Looking ahead, the window of opportunity for general-purpose platforms has closed, and growth will break through to both vertical and social ends. One end is the deep institutionalization of sports and macro derivatives, and the other end is the "financialization of attention" combined with the creator economy. The final outcome of the race will present a coexistence of high-frequency compliant data flow and long-tail social game. Disclaimer This article is for reference only. data.title: Can Monad Really Be Trusted? Is MON A Scam Or The Best Comeback Story?
data.description:
MON Debuts Below Presale, Triggering Immediate Investor Panic

Monad’s highly anticipated token launch has quickly shifted from excitement to frustration as MON opened below its pre-sale price and immediately pushed early buyers into the red.

The token debuted around $0.026 to $0.028 depending on the platform, only to drift even lower within hours of trading. A short-lived spike to $0.035 briefly raised hopes, but the rally collapsed just as quickly, reinforcing fears of aggressive insider selling.

Those concerns intensified as investors highlighted that 50% of the total MON supply is allocated to the team and insiders, a concentration many say creates structural sell pressure. Confidence eroded further when active participants who contributed to the Monad ecosystem for months found themselves receiving minimal rewards from the long-expected airdrop.

Thanks @monad for kebab.

🤡🤡🤡#MonadScam#monadpic.twitter.com/o11aR2wFxM

— PugOnly (@Pugonly) October 30, 2025

The sentiment swing was swift, driving the hashtag #MonadScam across social platforms and drawing comparisons to troubled launches like Berachain (BERA), another hyped Layer-1 that faced accusations of insider advantage and poor distribution.

Coinbase’s First ICO Sparks Debate

What makes the fallout even more notable is that Monad became Coinbase’s first ICO-style launch, an unusual move that even allowed U.S. investors to participate.

For many, Coinbase’s involvement initially felt like a stamp of credibility—one that implied a smoother debut and stronger price stability. That confidence has rapidly faded as MON continued trading below its pre-sale range, leaving early supporters questioning whether the exchange’s brand was enough to offset flawed tokenomics.

🚨 The ICO era is officially back — and this time, it’s backed by the big players!

🔹 Coinbase just launched a token sale platform for retail investors

🔥 First project: @monad — launching Nov 17 via Coinbase launchpad.

🔹According to Coinbase: We’re putting regular people… pic.twitter.com/SLRenX55Cc

— Danish TALK 🥷 (@Danishyt2255) November 10, 2025

Fueling the debate further is the coordinated support from major crypto players. Solana, Uniswap, and MetaMask all promoted Monad’s launch with branded visuals and public endorsements.

While some see this as validation that the project has serious backing, skeptics argue that the marketing push looked more like strategic positioning than genuine belief in the technology.

Let us not be sentimental and think logically.$Monad is coinbase FIRST ever token sale, and the US even gets to participate. You possibly can't think this doesn't do at least 100% profit.
One thing I'll advise is to bid something, even if it's $100 to have skin in the game. pic.twitter.com/cEQuyWw97j

— Xeusthegreat (♟,♟) (@SamuelXeus) November 10, 2025

With the token’s fundamentals still unclear and price action failing to stabilize, many investors remain unsure whether this support reflects insider familiarity—or merely an attempt to ride the hype cycle.

MON’s Recovery Depends on Utility, Trust, and Market Patience

As MON attempts to find its footing, its future remains uncertain. A recovery is possible—other tokens such as Pump.Fun eventually rebounded from shaky debuts and built meaningful ecosystems with real revenue and user activity.

Pump fun’s trading volume is up 21% in the last 6 hours! pic.twitter.com/A9bBqoM8lO

— Pump Fun News (@PumpfunNewsfeed) November 25, 2025

However, whether Monad can replicate that trajectory is far from guaranteed. The project has yet to demonstrate strong fundamentals or consistent usage, leaving the token’s value largely driven by speculation.

For now, Monad sits among the year’s most volatile and divisive launches. With early price weakness, community distrust over token distribution, and lingering doubts about insider concentration, the project must rebuild credibility before the market will give it a second look.

MON could rise to become one of 2025’s standout performers, but it could just as easily fade into the long list of hyped tokens that never lived up to expectations. Until clearer signals emerge, caution—not optimism—remains the market’s dominant stance.
